ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general octopus food product class; no specific entry for seasoned or marinated baby octopus exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers prepared octopus products; no specific code for Japanese-style seasoned baby octopus with red sauce exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for seasoned baby octopus as a prepared food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of seasoned baby octopus is approximately 100 g, equivalent to roughly 4–6 small whole octopuses with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Seasoned Baby Octopus — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Seasoned Baby Octopus (cr_id F03060030). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.0 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked octopus (USDA SR Legacy item 15165), as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seasoned baby octopus. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogy with mixed seafood dishes and protein-dominant prepared seafood items from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish has modest carbohydrate content (8.3 g/100 g) primarily from sauce ingredients; high protein (15.2 g/100 g) further blunts glycaemic response. GL per 100 g serving = (35 × 8.3) / 100 = 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 668 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 50 g or less per meal and request low-sodium soy sauce preparation, as a 100 g serving delivers 668 mg sodium — approximately 29% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35), modest carbohydrate (8.3 g/100 g), high protein (15.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seasoned baby octopus is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and blunts post-meal glucose response, and the estimated GI of 35 places it in the low-GI category.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (668 mg/100 g) offset by low saturated fat (~1 g/100 g) and lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lean protein and low saturated fat profile of octopus are cardioprotective, but the high sodium content (668 mg/100 g) poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should restrict portion size to 50 g and choose reduced-sodium preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory octopus protein and seasoning ingredients offset by high sodium and potential nightshade sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Octopus provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory potential, but the high sodium (668 mg/100 g) may worsen joint oedema and chilli/nightshade ingredients may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seasoned baby octopus is a Japanese-style prepared seafood dish consisting of small whole octopuses (typically Octopus vulgaris or Octopus minor) marinated and cooked in a savoury-spicy red sauce made from soy sauce, chilli, ginger, garlic, and sesame seeds. The dish is characterised by a tender, slightly chewy texture and a bold umami-forward flavour with moderate heat and a sesame aroma. It is commonly served as a side dish, appetiser, or banchan-style accompaniment in Japanese and Korean-influenced dining contexts in Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides 143 kcal per 100 g with a high protein content of 15.2 g, moderate fat of 5.5 g (predominantly unsaturated), and modest carbohydrate of 8.3 g. The primary clinical concern is the elevated sodium content of 668 mg/100 g, driven by soy sauce seasoning, which makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless portion-controlled or prepared with low-sodium alternatives. The estimated glycaemic index of approximately 35 (low) makes it suitable for people managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. Sesame, soy, and mollusc allergens are present.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in seasoned baby octopus?
Can diabetics eat seasoned baby octopus?
Is baby octopus high in protein?
Is seasoned octopus suitable for hypertension?
What allergens are in seasoned baby octopus?
How many calories in seasoned baby octopus?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03060030) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked octopus data as HPB does not separately report this field. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the only approved GI source; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in any approved source.

Saturated fat estimated at 1.0 g/100 g from USDA SR Legacy item 15165 (octopus, cooked, moist heat) as the closest approved-source equivalent; this is documented as an adaptation. The dietary tag 'plant_based' present in the input record has been removed as it is factually incorrect for an octopus-based seafood dish. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for seasoned baby octopus exists in any approved source.
